items.q: Why is Jupiter classified as a gas giant?
items.correct: Its bulk is mostly hydrogen and helium with no defined solid surface.
items.misconception: It is mostly air like Earth's atmosphere.
items.why: Students project Earth's atmosphere onto Jupiter. Jupiter has no rocky surface - it's gas transitioning to metallic hydrogen with depth. Very different from a thin atmosphere around a solid planet.

items.q: What is the difference between a solar eclipse and a lunar eclipse?
items.correct: In a solar eclipse the Moon blocks the Sun; in a lunar eclipse Earth's shadow covers the Moon.
items.misconception: Solar eclipses happen by day, lunar at night - that is the only difference.
items.why: Students give a superficial answer. The mechanism differs: solar = Moon between Sun and Earth (we're in Moon's shadow); lunar = Earth between Sun and Moon (Moon enters Earth's shadow).

items.q: What defines a star as being on the 'main sequence'?
items.correct: It fuses hydrogen into helium in its core.
items.misconception: It is the largest stage of a star's life.
items.why: Students think main sequence = most spectacular. It's defined by the FUEL: hydrogen fusion. Stars spend most of their lives there because hydrogen is plentiful and fusion is stable.

items.q: Which planet is the largest in the Solar System?
items.why: The answer examiners want is: Jupiter. “Saturn” is the distractor this question was built around — if it tempted you, that's the exact gap to close.

items.q: Where is the asteroid belt located?
items.why: It's Between Mars and Jupiter. “Beyond Neptune” is the classic near-miss here; knowing why it's wrong is worth as much as knowing the right answer.

items.q: How many planets are in our Solar System (excluding dwarf planets)?
items.why: Exact answer: 8. Estimation gets you near it; only the anchored number survives the exam hall.
